era_context: The late 8th century in Europe; Charlemagne's Frankish Empire is consolidating power following decades of military expansion. The Byzantine Empire is undergoing significant political change with Irene of Athens consolidating power. In the Islamic world, the Abbasid Caliphate under Harun al-Rashid is at its height. In East Asia, the Tang Dynasty continues under Emperor Dezong, while Japan's Heian period is beginning.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Frankish realm (sparse documentation)

events.title: Irene of Athens deposes her son Constantine VI in Byzantine Empire
events.description: In 797 CE, Irene of Athens, serving as regent for her young son Constantine VI, orchestrated his deposition and blinding. Constantine VI had attempted to assert his independence from his mother's regency, but Irene moved decisively to remove him from power. She had him blinded (a brutal punishment that rendered him unfit to rule) and imprisoned, then declared herself sole emperor. This act was controversial even in the Byzantine context, as it violated the principle of male succession and represented an unprecedented assertion of female imperial authority. Irene ruled as emperor (not merely regent) from 797 to 802 CE, making her one of the few women to hold supreme power in the Byzantine Empire. Her reign was marked by religious policies supporting icon veneration and diplomatic initiatives, but her authority was ultimately contested and short-lived.
events.certainty_note: Irene's deposition of Constantine VI is well-documented in Byzantine chronicles and administrative records. The date (797 CE) is secure.
cross_references: Related to 800-001 (Charlemagne's coronation, which occurred while Irene ruled)
Leads to 802-001 (Irene's deposition by Nicephorus I)

events.title: Charlemagne's Saxon campaigns continue; consolidation of northern territories
events.description: In 797 CE, Charlemagne continued his military campaigns against the Saxon peoples of northern Europe, a conflict that had begun in 772 CE. The Saxons resisted Frankish expansion and Christian conversion, and the campaigns were marked by brutal warfare. By 797 CE, Charlemagne had largely subdued Saxon resistance, though sporadic rebellions continued. The conquest of Saxony was one of Charlemagne's major military achievements and significantly expanded Frankish territorial control northward.
events.certainty_note: The Saxon campaigns are well-documented in Frankish chronicles; 797 CE falls within the documented period of these conflicts.
cross_references: Part of broader Saxon Wars (772–804 CE)

events.title: Harun al-Rashid's reign; Abbasid Caliphate at height of power
events.description: In 797 CE, Harun al-Rashid continued his rule of the Abbasid Caliphate, which was at the height of its power and cultural influence. The caliphate controlled vast territories from North Africa to Central Asia and maintained extensive trade networks. Harun al-Rashid's reign is remembered as a golden age of Islamic civilization, marked by cultural flourishing, administrative efficiency, and military strength. The caliphate's capital, Baghdad, was a major center of learning and commerce.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Harun al-Rashid's reign and the Abbasid Caliphate's power are well-documented in Islamic historical sources and numismatic evidence.
cross_references: Contemporary with 797-001 (Irene's deposition of Constantine VI)

events.title: Tang Dynasty under Emperor Dezong; continued administrative challenges
events.description: In 797 CE, Emperor Dezong of Tang continued his reign, facing ongoing challenges from regional military governors (jiedushi) whose power had grown during the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 CE). The Tang Dynasty maintained control over vast territories and continued to dominate East Asian politics, but central authority remained contested. The period saw continued cultural and technological advancement.
events.certainty_note: Tang Dynasty records confirm Dezong's reign and the general conditions of the empire in 797 CE.
cross_references: Part of broader Tang Dynasty period (618–907 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The blinding of Constantine VI is well-attested in Byzantine sources, but some modern scholars have questioned the extent of Irene's culpability and the degree to which she acted independently versus responding to political pressures from the Byzantine aristocracy. Additionally, the characterization of Irene's rule as a period of female empowerment may be anachronistic; Byzantine sources often emphasize her gender as a violation of proper order, and her authority was contested from the beginning. The sources also may exaggerate her role in religious policy decisions, particularly regarding icon veneration, which had broader support in Byzantine society.
historiographic_note: The year 797 CE is well-documented for Western Europe and the Byzantine Empire, with contemporary or near-contemporary chronicles providing detailed accounts of major political events. However, documentation is heavily weighted toward elite political and military history. For the Islamic world and Tang Dynasty, historical records are abundant but may reflect court biases. For regions outside these major civilizational centers, documentation is minimal. The sources also reflect the biases of literate elites and religious institutions; the experiences of common people are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Irene's rule in the Byzantine Empire was contemporary with Charlemagne's rise to power in the Frankish Empire, creating a complex geopolitical situation
Irene's deposition of Constantine VI led to her own deposition by Nicephorus I in 802 CE
The Saxon campaigns of 797 CE were part of the broader Saxon Wars (772–804 CE)
